coredata.dc:title: An ultrasensitive electrochemiluminescence assay for Hg2+ through graphene quantum dots and poly(5-formylindole) nanocomposite
coredata.dc:creator: Tang, Yun
Li, Jingjing
Guo, Qingfu
Nie, Guangming
coredata.dc:description: A novel and sensitive electrochemiluminescence (ECL) sensor based on poly(5-formylindole)/reduced graphene oxide (P5FIn/erGO) nanocomposite with high conductivity is fabricated for detection of Hg2+. Amino modified thymine (T)-riched single-strand (DNA S1) is connected with P5FIn/erGO by covalent coupling of the amino and aldehyde groups. In this sensor, Au nanoparticle (AuNP) linked graphene quantum dots (GQDs)-DNA S3 and DNA S2 is used as ECL signal probe. The presence of AuNP can increase the load of GQDs, and the combination of AuNP and GQDs can achieve signal amplification. The signal probe can be assembled to the modified electrode for hybridizing with DNA S1 in the presence of target Hg2+ through T-Hg2+-T coordination chemistry. The fabricated ECL sensor exhibits a wide linear response to Hg2+ ranging from 0.01 nM to 100 nM, and the detection limit is 2.48 pM. Moreover, this ECL sensor displays good stability, reproducibility and selectivity to Hg2+ against other interference ions, and can be utilized to detect Hg2+ in tap water and human serum samples with good performance.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrochemiluminescence
Graphene quantum dots
Poly(5-formylindole)
Nanocomposite
Hg2+

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst orcid primabst ref specialabst vitae

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Morphology characterization of erGO, P5FIn, P5FIn/erGO
Stability, reproducibility and selectivity of ECL sensor
Applications of the sensor in tap water and human serum samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the ECL sensor based on P5FIn/erGO nanocomposite and GQDs.
SEM images of (A) erGO, (B) P5FIn, (C) P5FIn/erGO.
(A) UV–vis absorption spectrum and the PL spectra of GQDs (inset), (B) TEM picture of the as-prepared GQDs.

(A) UV-vis absorption spectrum of (a) Linker DNA S3, (b) AuNP, (c) GQDs, (d) GQDs-DNA S3, (e) GQDs-DNA-AuNP in 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.4). (B) EIS characterization of electrode at different modified stages in 5 mM K3[Fe(CN)6]/K4[Fe(CN)6] containing 0.1M KCl. (a) Bare GCE (b) P5FIn/erGO/GCE (c) DNA S1/P5FIn/erGO/GEC (d) Hg2+/DNA S1/P5FIn/erGO/GCE (e) GQDs-DNA-AuNP/Hg2+/DNA S1/P5FIn/erGO/GCE.
Effects of (A) hybridization time and (B) reaction temperature on the response of the electrochemiluminescence sensor.

ECL profiles of the different concentration of Hg2+: the concentrations were as follows: (a) 100; (b) 50; (c) 10; (d) 1; (e) 0.1; (f) 0.05; (g) 0.01 nM. Inset: the linear relationship between ECL emission and the logarithm of Hg2+ concentration.

(A) Stability of the proposed ECL sensor toward Hg2+ (1.0 nM) under continuous 10-cyclic scans between 0 and −1.7 V in 0.05 mol L−1 Tris-HCl (pH 7.4) containing 0.1 mol L−1 K2S2O8. Scan rare: 100 mV s−1, photomultiplier tube voltage: 800 V. (B) The reproducibility tests of five modified electrodes to test of Hg2+ (1.0 nM). (C) Selectivity studies corresponding to the analysis of Hg2+ and Zn2+, Fe3+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Ag+, Cu2+, Pb2+. All of the concentrations of these metal ions were 1.0 nM.

head.ce:title: An ultrasensitive electrochemiluminescence assay for Hg2+ through graphene quantum dots and poly(5-formylindole) nanocomposite

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Optic-Electric Sensing and Analytical Chemistry for Life Science, MOE, Shandong Key Laboratory of Biochemical Analysis, Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Life Science in Universities of Shandong, College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, Qingdao University of Science and Technology, Qingdao, 266042, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Optic-Electric Sensing and Analytical Chemistry for Life Science
Shandong Key Laboratory of Biochemical Analysis
Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Life Science in Universities of Shandong
College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering
Qingdao University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A sandwich-type electrochemiluminescence sensor based on poly(5-formylindole)/reduced graphene oxide nanocomposite is firstly fabricated for sensitive determination of Hg2+, which has a low detection limit, excellent stability and good selectivity.

A novel and sensitive electrochemiluminescence (ECL) sensor based on poly(5-formylindole)/reduced graphene oxide (P5FIn/erGO) nanocomposite with high conductivity is fabricated for detection of Hg2+. Amino modified thymine (T)-riched single-strand (DNA S1) is connected with P5FIn/erGO by covalent coupling of the amino and aldehyde groups. In this sensor, Au nanoparticle (AuNP) linked graphene quantum dots (GQDs)-DNA S3 and DNA S2 is used as ECL signal probe. The presence of AuNP can increase the load of GQDs, and the combination of AuNP and GQDs can achieve signal amplification. The signal probe can be assembled to the modified electrode for hybridizing with DNA S1 in the presence of target Hg2+ through T-Hg2+-T coordination chemistry. The fabricated ECL sensor exhibits a wide linear response to Hg2+ ranging from 0.01 nM to 100 nM, and the detection limit is 2.48 pM. Moreover, this ECL sensor displays good stability, reproducibility and selectivity to Hg2+ against other interference ions, and can be utilized to detect Hg2+ in tap water and human serum samples with good performance.

ce:section.ce:para: In the mid-20th century, a large-scale methylmercury poisoning incident was happened in Minamata, a seaside town in Japan, causing serious influences on environment and human health. The devastating impact of Hg2+ started to attract the world’s attention and the United Nations Environment Programme (UNEP) adopted a new international convention named Minamata convention to control and reduce mercury emissions [1,2]. It is widely known that Hg2+ is one of the most hazardous heavy metallic pollutants, which comes from natural or anthropogenic sources, such as chemical manufacturing, fossil fuel combustion, and so forth [3,4]. The emitted mercury can be readily transferred from the environment into food chain and accumulated in the human body, and thus causing severe damage to the brain, nervous system, endocrine system, kidneys and other organs [5,6]. Thus, the development of highly selective and sensitive Hg2+ detection method is of considerable importance.

Up to now, many traditional methods have been reported for detecting Hg2+, including inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) [7], graphite furnace atomic absorption spectrometry (GFAAS) [8], surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) [9], atomic fluorescence spectrometry (AFS) [10]. Although these technologies are reliable to realize the analysis of Hg2+, most of them required expensive instrumentation or tedious sample preparation. These shortcomings dramatically restrict their applications in daily monitoring. Thus, it is a growing need to explore low-cost, portable and simple sensing platform for rapid Hg2+ determination.

Sensing strategy has attracted much interest owing to their great versatility, although there is no evidence to prove which method is superior. It does not require intricate instruments and complex sample preparation processes. So far, there are some sensors developed for Hg2+ measurement. Gu et al. have designed and synthesized novel thiazole derivative-modified upconversion nanoparticles probes for Hg2+ detection in living cells, and this group also summarized chemosensors for Hg2+ based on azaacenes active elements, which have excellent selectivity and sensitivity [11,12]. Previous literature reported that Hg2+ possessed a special property to interact with two DNA thymine bases (T) and form a stable T-Hg2+-T complex [13], which provided a simple way to realize the rapid determination of Hg2+. Actually, due to the specific recognition ability of Hg2+ with two T base pairs in DNA duplexes, many sensing strategies for Hg2+ detection based on electrochemistry [14], fluorescence [15], colorimetry [16] have been constructed over the years. Although the fluorometric method is sensitive, it still requires sophisticated instruments. Electrochemical technology has a relatively complex pretreatment process though it has an excellent flexibility. Colorimetric assays can be operated at room temperature, showing simple and rapid properties, but their sensitivity is not high.

By contrast, electrochemiluminescence (ECL), also known as electrogenerated chemiluminescence, is virtually an electrochemical redox reaction that takes place at the electrode and is controlled by a certain voltage [17]. The clever combination of electrochemistry and chemiluminescence brings ECL many unique advantages [18–20]. Firstly, electrochemiluminescence (ECL) is relatively stable and convenient, and has displayed good sensitivity, selectivity and strong anti-interference ability. Secondly, the ECL detection device is simple and easy to operate and can be used together with chromatography and electrophoresis to realize ultrasensitive and accurate determination of heavy metal ions. Thirdly, ECL reaction can be optimized by majorizing the electrode material, size and position, as well as the biocatalytic, electrocatalytic and other regeneration cycle processes, and further increase the analytical sensitivity. All of the good features of ECL technology make it to be a powerful analysis tool for heavy metal ions detection. Notably, multifarious sensors taking advantages of T-Hg2+-T specificity and ECL technologies have been reported for Hg2+ analysis with good performance [21–23]. However, to our knowledge, there is currently no report on the quantitative detection of Hg2+ using the ECL method based on conducting polymer nanocomposite. Conducting polymer nanocomposite shows high conductivity and large specific surface area, which can increase the loading capacity of DNA and enhance the sensitivity of sensor.

The key challenge of ECL sensor is to find new luminescent materials and investigate the relevance to the targets. The traditional ECL luminophores, such as Ru(bpy)3 2+ and luminol are usually expensive, instable and difficult to label, which restricts their extensive applications [24]. As a new type of carbon material, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are expected to be a superior ECL emitter candidate in recent years owing to their intrinsic properties, including environmental friendliness, low toxicity, good biocompatibility, low cost, easy labeling, high ECL activity and unusual electro-optical properties [25,26]. The carboxyl group and hydroxyl group functional groups are distributed on the surface and edges of GQDs, which can be easily combined with other bioactive substances. Moreover, compared to graphene nanosheets and nanoribbons, zero-dimensional graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have stronger quantum confinement and boundary effects due to their lateral dimension below 10 nm, and thus GQDs have a more attractive application prospects in many areas, such as solar photovoltaic devices, biomedicine, light-emitting diodes and sensor [27]. Gold nanoparticle (AuNP) is an important material in nanoscience due to the special physical and chemical properties. The presence of AuNP can increase the load of GQDs, and thus significantly increase the electroluminescent intensity of the modified electrode. Furthermore, high concentration of AuNP can enhance the pre-enrichment of Hg2+ [28], so the combination of AuNP and GQDs can enhance the sensitivity of Hg2+ detection. As far as we know, there is no literature concerning the AuNP linked GQDs-based ECL sensor for Hg2+ detection. In this work, we introduced single-stranded DNA with amino and sulfhydryl groups at each end to bind GQDs and AuNP respectively, achieving the link of AuNP and GQDs. The GQDs-DNA-AuNP nanoprobe can realize double signal amplification due to the combined advantages of AuNP and GQDs.

Electrode modified material is another important factor affecting the sensitivity of the sensor. Conducting polymers (CPs), acting as immobilization carriers and signal transduction of sensors, have been extensively investigated due to their favorable properties such as good conductivity, biocompatibility and high sensitivity [29,30]. As widely investigated conducting polymers, polyindole have shown promising application in sensors because of the high redox activity, good stability and special structure properties [31]. When functional groups are introduced into the indole monomer, the structure and properties of the polymer can be adjusted and the immobilization of the bioactive substance can be also enhanced [32]. Poly(5-formylindole) (P5FIn) has loose porous network structure and good optoelectronic properties, which can directly link with amino-functioned DNA to form imine bond though nucleophilic reaction [33]. Graphene oxide (GO) is a widely used nanomaterial with large surface area and strong π-π* conjunction [34]. Reduced graphene oxide (erGO) was prepared by electrochemical reduction of GO. The excellent properties of erGO, such as high conductivity, good biocompatibility, easy to immobilize many bioactive molecules, make it to be an attractive material to composite with polyindole [35]. Our team has reported a sandwich-type ECL immunosensor based on P5FIn/erGO nanocomposite, which can realize ultrasensitive determination of carcinoembryonic antigen [36].

Based on these considerations, herein, a novel ECL sensor for trace Hg2+ detection was constructed using P5FIn/erGO nanocomposite as ECL substrate and GQDs-DNA-AuNP as the signal probe. Amino-modified DNA S1 was immobilized on P5FIn/erGO modified electrode through amide bond, followed by adding Hg2+ into the solution. T-Hg2+-T complex was formed through the connection of two ssDNA and Hg2+, and thus realized the ultrasensitive determination of Hg2+. The ECL sensor showed good sensitivity and selectivity, which can be utilized for monitoring of Hg2+ from actual tap water and human serum samples with satisfactory results.
The reagents and instruments used in this experiment were provided in the Supporting Information.

GQDs were obtained by citric acid carbonation method according to the former reported literature with a few minor changes [37]. ECL signal probe (denoted GQDs-S3) was prepared referring to the literature previously [38]. 60 mg EDC and 60 mg NHS were placed into 5 mL GQDs solution and stirred 30 min to activate the surface carboxylic group of GQDs. Amine modified DNA S3 was transported to the GQDs solution, followed by stirring for 24 h at room temperature to form GQDs-S3 probe through condensation reaction. Finally, the solution was washed three times with ethanol to remove the unbound DNA S3.

ECL nanoprobe (denoted GQDs-DNA-AuNP) was obtained as follows: TCEP was diverted to DNA solution and stayed for 20 min so as to inhibit the formation of disulfide bond. The prepared Ru-S3 and DNA S2 were transported to 1 mL fresh AuNP in the ratio of 1:90. The concentrations of SDS and PBS were adjusted to 0.01% and 0.01 M, respectively. The mixture were subjected to incubate for 20 min at room temperature and then increase the concentration of NaCl to 0.05 M by using 2 M NaCl, while the concentration of PBS and SDS were kept constant. After incubating in the dark overnight, the resulting solution was washed and dispersed in 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.4).

Hg2+ can effectively adjust the base T mismatch to form a T-Hg2+-T structure that is even more stable than the Watson-Crick A-T base pair [39]. The mechanism of this experiment is mainly on the basis of the pairing of oligonucleotide chain. Three oligonucleotide strands were used in this work, in which DNA S1 and DNA S2 were T-rich structures, so that DNA S1 and DNA S2 can be connected by Hg2+, and thus the ECL nanoprobe was modified to the electrode surface. The specific construction steps of ECL sensor are described at Scheme 1 .

Before electrochemical polymerization, GCE was polished by 1.0 μm, 0.3 μm and 0.05 μm α-Al2O3, respectively. Then GCE was ultrasonicated and rinsed with DDW, 95% ethanol and DDW in sequence. Firstly, P5FIn/erGO nanocomposite was deposited on the electrode surface by electrodeposition. The specific preparation method referred to our previously published literature. Then, the P5FIn/erGO modified electrode was incubated into 0.1 μM DNA S1 (PBS, pH 6.0) for 1 h at room temperature to obtain DNA S1/P5FIn/erGO/GCE. After 5 μL Hg(NO3)2 solution with different concentrations was dropped on the electrode, 10 μL ECL nanoprobe solution was dropped to the modified electrode and incubated at 37 °C for 80 min. As a result, ECL probe was attached to the electrode surface through the connection of DNA S1 and DNA S2, thus realizing the detection of Hg2+ by ECL method.

The modified electrodes were taken in 0.05 M Tris-HCl (pH 7.4) containing 0.1 M K2S2O8 and 0.1 M KCl for a typical ECL test. The ECL intensity correlated with the mercury concentrations could be measured by a MPI-A ECL analyzer. The linear scan potential was used from -1.7 V to 0 V with a scan rate of 100 mV s−1. The voltage of the photomultiplier tube (PMT) was operated at 800 V.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology characterization of erGO, P5FIn, P5FIn/erGO
ce:section.ce:para: The surface and structure information of erGO, P5FIn and P5FIn/erGO nanocomposite were characterized by scanning electron microscope (SEM) images. It can be seen from Fig. 1 A, a lamellar structure with trivial drape of erGO was presented. As depicted in Fig. 1B, P5FIn film showed loose multihole reticulation. When 5FIn was polymerized to erGO, P5FIn/erGO nanocomposite presented a nanostructure with loose porous and intact fibers (Fig. 1C), indicating that the P5FIn was successfully composited with erGO. The loose porous structure of P5FIn/erGO nanocomposite can accelerate ion transport during the redox process, thereby elevating electrochemical properties of electrode. Meanwhile, this structure was able to enlarge the specific surface area and consequently improve the loading capacity of DNA S1. In summary, the excellent morphology properties of nanocomposite make it to be a promising material for the development of CPs-based sensors.

The Ultraviolet-visible (UV-vis) absorption spectrum, photoluminescence (PL) spectra and transmission electron microscope (TEM) were used to characterize the as-prepared GQDs. The UV–vis absorption spectrum of GQDs displayed a strong absorption band at about 360 nm (Fig. 2 A, curve a), however there was no apparent absorption of citric acid (Fig. 2A, curve b), which indicated the successfully synthesized of GQDs. The inset of Fig. 2A exhibited the PL spectra of GQDs. The maximum excitation and emission wavelengths of the prepared GQDs were appeared at around 360 and 460 nm, respectively. The height of the absorption peak of GQDs was almost the same as the height of the excitation peak, which implied that little non-emission absorption had been generated in GQDs. The result was similar to previously reported of GQDs [37]. The TEM image illustrated that the as-prepared GQDs were monodisperse in solution, and the size was about 20 nm (Fig. 2B). In addition, the collected GQDs exhibited strong ECL intensity and magnificent stability, which was conducive to the development of an electrochemical luminescence sensor.

UV-vis absorption spectrum was utilized to prove the successful formation of GQDs-DNA-AuNP nanoprobe. The absorption peaks at around 260 nm, 519 nm and 360 nm were corresponded to the characteristic peaks of DNA, AuNP and GQDs, respectively (Fig. 3 A, curve a, b, c). After DNA S3 attached on the surface of GQDs, two typical peaks were exhibited toward DNA S3 and GQDs (Fig. 3A, curve d). Particularly, the absorption peaks occurred slight shift, probably owing to the dehydration condensation reaction of amino group modified on DNA S3 and carboxyl group on the surface of GQDs, which lead to the n-π* transitions of nanoprobe. When AuNP and DNA S2 were added to the GQDs-DNA S3 solution (Fig. 3A, curve e), three special absorption peaks were appeared, which were corresponded to DNA, GQDs and AuNP, indicating the successfully combination of GQDs, DNA and AuNP. These results obtained above showed the successful preparation of ECL nanoprobe.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) is an excellent tool to explore interfacial electrical properties of modified electrode. The inherent performance of materials or some particular process might influence the interfacial properties of an electrochemical system, which can be illustrated by EIS [40]. Therefore, EIS was suitable to characterize the different stages of sensor fabrication process. The impedance spectroscopy includes a linear portion and a semicircular portion. The linear portion at the lower frequency on behalf of the diffusion process, and the semicircle at the higher frequency on behalf of the electron transfer resistance (Ret). In this experiment, the EIS characterization of the electrode at different stages was measured in 5 mM electroactive probes ([Fe(CN)6]3−/4−) containing 0.1 M KCl with the frequency range from 0.1 Hz to 10,000 Hz. As showed in Fig. 3B, compared with bare GCE (curve a), the Ret value decreased obviously after the modification of P5FIn/erGO nanocomposite on the bare electrode (curve b). This kind of electrochemical response variation was due to the good redox activity and electrochemical nature of the as-prepared nanocomposite, which accelerated electron-transporting capability. Meantime, the result demonstrated that P5FIn/erGO was successfully deposited on the surface of the electrode. As DNA S1 was fixed on the P5FIn/erGO/GCE (curve c), the Ret value increased distinctly, the reason for this was the oligonucleotide chain with negative charge hindered the redox reaction, leading to the electron transfer harder than before. The Ret value had slightly decreased when Hg2+ was assembled onto the electrode (curve d). This phenomenon was because the addition of Hg2+ made DNA S1 to be vertical, which facilitated the formation of double-stranded [41] and promoted electron transfer rate of electroactive probes to the electrode surface. When the modified electrode immersed in the ECL nanoprobe solution (curve e), the Ret value of the electrode was continuously decreased. The reason for the decreased of Ret value was mainly for the following three reasons: Firstly, the double-stranded DNA, came from the connected between DNAS1 and DNA S2 via Hg2+, can enhance the electron transfer rate between the solution and the electrode surface due to the rigidity property of DNA duplex. Secondly, the presence of AuNP not only greatly increased the immobilized amount of DNA, but also increased the electron transfer rate. Thirdly, GQDs had extraordinary conductivity and electrocatalytic activity which further accelerated electron transfer. In short, all of these results demonstrated that the process of assembling the ECL sensor was successful and able to be used in the sensor.

In order to have an ideal determination to the target analyte, it was generally necessary to optimize the conditions of the reaction. When DNA was involved in the reaction for a sensor, the time of the hybridization and the reaction temperature were critical parameters for the ECL test. Here, 1.0 nM Hg2+ was adopted to explore the optimal condition of this sensor. As seen in Fig. 4 A, with the increase of hybridization time from 0 min to 80 min, the ECL intensity increased continuously and reached equilibrium after 80 min, so 80 min was chosen as hybridization time of DNA. Fig. 4B demonstrated the relation between ECL intensity and reaction temperature. The ECL intensity enhanced with the raise of the reaction temperature and achieved the maximum at 37 °C and then decreased. Thus, 37 °C was chosen as the optimum temperature.

According to the ECL detection mechanism, DNA S1, DNA S2 and Hg2+ can form T-Hg2+-T structure after immobilizing Hg2+ and ECL nanoprobe on the surface of modified electrode, so GQDs was attached to the electrode at the same time. The ECL intensity from GQDs was related to the concentration of Hg2+, and thus the concentration of Hg2+ can be determined by our prepared sensor platform. Under optimal conditions, the concentration-dependent ECL profiles were evaluated by examining a series of standard solutions containing different Hg2+ concentrations.

As shown in Fig. 5 , the ECL intensity decreased gradually with the decrease of the concentration of Hg2+. The inset of Fig. 5 was the linear relationship between ECL emission and the logarithm of Hg2+ concentration. The linear equation was Y = 7928.66 + 2583.75 lgc(Hg2+) with a range of 0.01–100 nM and the correlation coefficient was 0.9959. The detection limit was about 2.48 pM after calculating by 3σ method [42]. Compared with other reported sensors (Table S1), the sensor we constructed had a lower detection limit and a relatively larger detection range, indicating the higher sensitivity of this ECL sensor. The reason for the lower detection limit was mainly as follow: (1) AuNP had the effect of similar wires or electronic channels to speed up the electronic transmission rate and enhanced the loading capacity of ssDNA. (2) GQDs had a similar structure to graphene, with high ECL intensity and good conductivity, which can achieve the amplification of ECL signal. (3) P5FIn/erGO nanocomposite combined the advantages of graphene and conductive polymers, which provided larger specific surface area and facilitated the immobilization of bioactive molecules. The combination of the three factors not only accelerated the transfer rate of electrons, but also enhanced the surface area of the electrode, thereby increasing the electronic transmission capability of the sensor and improving the sensitivity.

The ECL intensity responses and titration curve of modified electrode was measured through dropping different concentrations of Hg2+ in 0.1 M K2S2O8 and 0.05 M Tris-HCl solution to evaluate the detection ability of this sensor for a trace amount of Hg2+. As shown in Fig. S1A, upon increasing the concentration of Hg2+, the ECL intensity gradually increased and finally achieved a plateau when the Hg2+ concentration was more than 300 nM due to the saturation of DNA binding site. This result indicated that Hg2+ can be tested directly in ECL test solution with good performance. Fig. S1B was the corresponding titration curve plotted using the data collected during the ECL titration of GQDs at various Hg2+ concentrations. According to the inset of Fig. S1B, the ECL intensity showed a linear correlation with the concentration of Hg2+ in the range of 0.01–300 nM (Y = 7455.49 + 2611.82 lgc(Hg2+), R = 0.9921). The result was slightly different from the data of Fig. 5, which was obtained by layer drip method. There are may be two reasons for this difference. Firstly, the electrode construction environment is different. The Hg2+ was directly combined with T pairs in electrode surface of Fig. 5. In Fig. S1, the coordination reaction of Hg2+ and probe was completed in solution. Secondly, there was some ion interference to a certain extent in the test solution, which also may cause some slight decrease of correlation coefficient.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability, reproducibility and selectivity of ECL sensor
ce:section.ce:para: Stability and reproducibility were important parameters for ECL sensor application. The stability was assessed using the same modified electrode to test the ECL response. As showed in Fig. 6 A, the ECL intensities were high and did not change with the change of scanning cycles. The relative standard deviation (RSD) was 0.48% (n = 10), which indicated that this sensor had good cycling stability and facilitated the ECL detection. Under the same experimental conditions, five proposed electrodes were used to detect the reproducibility of the ECL sensor (Fig. 6B). The calculated RSD was 2.95%, indicating a relatively good reproducibility.

To demonstrate the feasibility of the prepared sensor for actual sample determination, we should research the selectivity of this sensor. The sensor was incubated in various potential interferences, including Zn2+, Fe3+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Ag+, Cu2+ (the concentration of the above ions was 1.0 nM, respectively) and the mixed solution of the above ions. It can be observed from Fig. 6C that only in the presence of Hg2+ had the highest ECL value, and the other interfering ions had weak ECL signal. When examining the mixed solution of the above ions, the ECL intensity did not change significantly compared with the measurement of Hg2+. These results illustrated that the prepared sensor had good selectivity to Hg2+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Applications of the sensor in tap water and human serum samples
ce:section.ce:para: To further explore the potential applications of this constructed sensor in vivo or in vitro experiments, standard addition method was used to evaluate the analytical performance of Hg2+ in tap water and human serum samples, respectively. Hg2+ solutions with different concentrations were added into tap water and human serum sample before detecting. Then, these treated solutions were acted as the actual sample for the recovery experiment. As showed in Table S2, the recovery experiments provided the acceptable data of the samples in tap water with recoveries between 98.6% and 100.8% for the Hg2+, and the corresponding RSDs were ranged from 1.5% to 4.5%. In human serum sample, the recoveries were in the range of 99.6–107.5% with the RSDs ranged from 2.5% to 5.0%. These results indicated that this constructed sensor had good applicability for actual sample detection and might have certain potential applications in the in vivo or in vitro area.

P5FIn/erGO nanocomposite and GQDs-DNA-AuNP nanoprobe was successfully used to construct a sensitive ECL sensor for determination of Hg2+. The loose porous structure of P5FIn/erGO nanocomposite can accelerate ion transport during the redox process and significantly improve the loading capacity of DNA S1 due to the increase of the specific surface area. The presence of AuNP can increase the load of GQDs, and enhance the signal amplification capability of the signal probe. The prepared ECL sensor had a wide linear range from 0.01 nM to 100 nM, with a low limit of 2.48 pM. The results also showed the ECL sensor had good stability, selectivity and reproducibility. In addition, the ECL sensor can be used to detect Hg2+ in actual tap water and human serum sample with good performance. This ECL sensor not only provides a simple and convenient method for Hg2+ detection, but also broadens the application of P5FIn/erGO nanocomposite in environmental monitoring. This proposed ECL strategy based on conducting polyindole derivatives provides a potential application to detect the other heavy metal pollutant in the clinical, food and environmental monitoring fields.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51373089), NSF of Shandong (ZR2011BM003) and Talent Fund of QUST (2018).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2018.11.151.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The minamata convention on mercury: a first step toward protecting future generations
Minamata convention on mercury: a contemporary reminder
Recent advances on functionalized upconversion nanoparticles for detection of small molecules and ions in biosystems
Three-dimensional paper-based electrochemiluminescence device for simultaneous detection of Pb2+ and Hg2+ based on potential-control technique

GFAAS determination of mercury in muscle samples of fish from Amazon, Brazil
Ultrasensitive SERS detection of mercury based on the assembled gold nanochains

Azaacenes as active elements for sensing and bio applications
Highly selective oligonucleotide-based sensor for mercury(II) in aqueous solutions
DNA modified Fe3O4@Au magnetic nanoparticles as selective probes for simultaneous detection of heavy metal ions

Colorimetric detection of mercury(II) based on gold nanoparticles, fluorescent gold nanoclusters and other gold-based nanomaterials
Ir(iii) complex-based phosphorescence and electrochemiluminescence chemodosimetric probes for Hg(ii) ions with high selectivity and sensitivity
Enhanced electrochemiluminescence behavior of gold-silver bimetallic nanoclusters and its sensing application for mercury(II)
A facile and sensitive electrochemiluminescence biosensor for Hg2+ analysis based on a dual-function oligonucleotide probe
Ultrasensitive electrochemiluminescence detection of mercury ions based on DNA oligonucleotides and cysteamine modified gold nanoparticles probes
Sensitive detection of Hg2+ with switchable electrochemiluminescence luminophore and disposable bipolar electrode
Valence states effect on electrogenerated chemiluminescence of gold nanocluster
Glowing graphene quantum dots and carbon dots: properties, syntheses, and biological applications
Electrochemiluminescence of nitrogen- and sulfur-doped graphene quantum dots
Boron-and nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots/graphene hybrid nanoplatelets as efficient electro catalysts for oxygen reduction
Gold nanoparticles decorated carbon fiber mat as a novel sensing platform for sensitive detection of Hg(II)
Simple label-free femtomolar DNA detection based on a nanostructure composite material: MWNT-doped poly(indole-6-carboxylic acid)
Direct synthesis of highly conductive poly(3,4- ethylenedioxythiophene):poly(4-styrenesulfonate) (PEDOT:PSS)/graphene composites and their applications in energy harvesting systems
Electrochemical immunosensor for the carcinoembryonic antigen based on a nanocomposite consisting of reduced graphene oxide, gold nanoparticles and poly(indole-6-carboxylic acid)

An electrochemical immunosensor for the tumor marker α-fetoprotein using a glassy carbon electrode modified with a poly(5-formylindole), single-wall carbon nanotubes, and coated with gold nanoparticles and antibody
Electrochemiluminescence biosensor based on conducting poly(5-formylindole) for sensitive detection of Ramos cells

Electrogenerated chemiluminescence resonance energy transfer between Ru(bpy)3 2+ electrogenerated chemiluminescence and gold nanoparticles/graphene oxide nanocomposites with graphene oxide as coreactant and its sensing application
Ultrasensitive and simultaneous detection of heavy metal ions based on three-dimensional graphene-carbon nanotubes hybrid electrode materials
A graphene quantum dots based electrochemiluminescence immunosensor for carcinoembryonic antigen detection using poly(5-formylindole)/reduced graphene oxide nanocomposite
Blue luminescent graphene quantum dots and graphene oxide prepared by tuning the carbonization mdegree of citric acid
DNA methyltransferase activity detection based on graphenequantum dots using fluorescence and fluorescence anisotropy
Interaction of mercury (II) with the DNA dodecamer CGCGAATTCGCG. A 1H and 15N NMR study
Invertase-labeling gold-dendrimer for in situ amplified detection mercury(II) with glucometer readout and thymine-Hg2+-thymine coordination chemistry
Limit of blank, limit of detection and limit of quantitation

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Yun Tang is a graduate student in the College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, Qingdao University of Science and Technology. She received her B.E. degree in 2016 from Qingdao University of Science and Technology. Her current research interests focus on the field of design and application of electrochemiluminescence sensors based on conducting polymer.

Jingjing Li is currently a graduate student from Qingdao University of Science and Technology. She received her B.E. degree in 2016. Her current research interests focus on material chemical engineering, including synthesis inorganic/organic nanocomposite materials and its electrochemical and electrochromic properties study.

Qingfu Guo is a Ph.D. student in the College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering at Qingdao University of Science and Technology. He received his master degree from Qingdao University of Science and Technology in 2010. His current research interests focus on the preparation of conducting polymer nanocomposites and their applications in the field of sensors.

Guangming Nie is currently an associate professor at College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, Qingdao University of Science and Technology, China. He received his Ph.D. in applied chemistry from Qingdao University of Science and Technology in 2010. Afterwards he worked as a postdoctor in Technical Institute of Physics and Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences and as a visiting scientist in Nanyang Technological University. His current research interests focus on Nano electrochemistry, including sensor and energy storage device based on nano-conducting polymers.
